Hi -- thanks for the code! With respect to the original question, I was able to get this to play well with keras DataGenerator by using the preprocessing_function option. It has some limitations with respect to which Augmentations can be used, but it worked fo the cases I was trying. 

```
from imgaug import augmenters as iaa

aug1 = iaa.GaussianBlur(sigma=(0, 2.0))
aug2 = iaa.AdditiveGaussianNoise(scale=0.01 * 255)

def additional_augmenation(image):
    image = aug1.augment_image(image)
    image = aug2.augment_image(image)
    return image
```

This additional function can be passed to keras data generator as a pre-processing function.

```
datagen = ImageDataGenerator(rotation_range=20, zoom_range=0.2,
                                    preprocessing_function=additional_augmenation)
```

One caveat to note is the following from Keras documentation regarding preprocessing_function: 

> function that will be implied on each input. The function will run after the image is resized and augmented.  The function should take one argument: one image (Numpy tensor with rank 3), and should output a Numpy tensor with the same shape.

So, this approach may not work for all the augmentations.

It is not recommended to generate CIGAR for read overlapping because 1) generating CIGAR for every overlap is very slow; 2) it is usually not necessary to have cigar for read overlapping. SAM is also the wrong format for read overlapping. No read overlappers output SAM.

On your example, minimap/minimap2 ignore anchors with the same position if the read name is the same, so you shouldn't see perfect alignment. However, with CIGAR on, alignment extension may still produce a nearly perfect alignment from different seeds. This is a problem with minimap2, which I will try to fix at some point. Thanks.

Unfortunately, TOML and JSON are not equivalent. In this particular case, you're bumping into TOML's inability to represent heterogeneous arrays. For specs, see https://github.com/toml-lang/toml#user-content-array, especially the sentence "Data types may not be mixed [...]".

If you're searching for a human-readable format that is also expressive, I recommend CSON, the Coffeescript version of JSON. Which (shameless plug :) ) I also happen to have a python implementation for: https://github.com/avakar/pycson.

API Gateway is pretty big, so we'd probably want to identify the subset to support initially.  I think the format above is reasonable, and the most important features to me are:

* Ability to map HTTP paths and methods to the lambda
* Ability to map responses to HTTP status codes
* Ability to map response HTTP headers (e.g. Content-Type or Location)
* Probably some way of mapping kapp's 'environment' name to the API Gateway 'stage'
  * This part is a little fuzzy to me

Perhaps other folks with more API Gateway knowledge could chime in.  I think it may be easier to start from scratch rather than try to finish the `restapi.py` work that's already in the tree, but I could be wrong.

I would love to work with you to figure out the best way to do this.  Unfortunately, 99% of the tests are functional, and I broke out the PDFs they use into the separate static_pdfs repository.  That's 24MB, and it's easy for me to believe you need those to test the packaging, but at the same time, that's a lot of cruft for a release tarball.

SO, what I did on this release:

- The release to PyPI doesn't even contain the tests (because they won't work without the PDFs).

- I clicked the button on github to make a corresponding release (tag v0.2).  Github puts all the files in the repository in that release, so it includes the tests, but not the data they need.

- If you extract the files from the github release, you will find there is a .travis.yml file, which describes how the automated tests are run, and has code to grab all the dependencies:

  - "git clone https://github.com/pmaupin/static_pdfs tests/static_pdfs"
install:
  - "pip install ."
  - "pip install reportlab || true"
  - "pip install zlib || true"
  - "pip install unittest2 || true"

Maybe the right answer is to start making releases for static_pdfs, and modify the code to grab one of those and test with it rather than to simply do a git clone?

In any case, if it would help, I can give you write access to the github repo as well, so you can play with things that would make your life easier.

Or maybe I'm just being old-fashioned and 24MB is no big deal.  Please let me know what you think.

Thanks,

Hi Daniel,

In principle I'm happy to support the surface alignments, but I haven't seen much description of them. Nathan Schneider's PEG has this:

```
ALIGNMENT = "~" ~r"[A-Za-z0-9.,]+"
```

which looks pretty free-form to me (`~.Ab,,,C.1` is valid?). In the examples in his amr.py, I only see two variants: `~e.N`, where N is a token index (0-based), and `~e.M,N`, where M,N is a list of token indices (also 0-based). Assuming the latter can have more than just 2 indices, this means there's really one form, something like:

```
ALIGNMENT = "~e." ~r"[0-9]+" ("," ~r"[0-9]"+)*
```

Finally, I'm confused as to what it means to align relations (edges) to tokens. E.g., in the example from the README, `~e.1` seems redundant on `:polarity` and I'm not sure why `~e.0` is on `:mode`:

```
>>> a = AMR("(h / hug-01~e.2 :polarity~e.1 -~e.1 :ARG0 (y / you~e.3) :ARG1 y \
             :mode~e.0 imperative~e.5 :result (s / silly-01~e.4 :ARG1 y))", \
            "Do n't hug yourself silly !".split())
>>> a
(h / hug-01~e.2[hug] :polarity~e.1[n't] -~e.1[n't]
    :ARG0 (y / you~e.3[yourself])
    :ARG1 y
    :mode~e.0[Do] imperative~e.5[!]
    :result (s / silly-01~e.4[silly]
        :ARG1 y))
```

But still, I have not seen these alignments in any gold data, papers about AMR, or the dozen or so AMR-related code projects I've browsed (besides the [amr-hackathon](https://github.com/nschneid/amr-hackathon) one, of course). The only other notation for token alignment's I've seen is the [JAMR-style metadata string](https://github.com/clic-lab/amr/blob/07b581e7d160fa2625eeefa86ae5e9fe5c589be2/utils/jamr/docs/Alignment_Format.md), e.g.:

```
# ::tok Saudi Arabia ( SA )
# ::alignments 0-2|0+0.0+0.0.0+0.0.1
(c / country
  :name (n / name
          :op1 "Saudi"
          :op2 "Arabia"))
```

@nschneid, can you please confirm if the more restrictive `ALIGNMENT` rule above is accurate/sufficient? And you call them "ISI-style alignments"; does that mean there is some ISI document that describes them?

Thank you for any clarification.

`range("B2")` refers to a specific cell, so this would be ambiguous as to whether or not you want to write the array to the cell or to the range. Additionally, the motivation for the end param is to allow merges, since you can do `range("B2", "C3").merge()`. An implicit end parameter would not make much sense here, as `range("B2").merge()` would be inconsistent with the proposed API.

That being said, if you do want to use an implicit end parameter specifically for your use case, you could use `range("B2", (float('inf'), float('inf')))`, in other words, pass a tuple of coordinates as the end parameter. I believe assigning a smaller array to a larger range works, is that sufficient for your use case?

The re-organisation sounds good. I think it needs to be impartial regarding which packager to use. Pehaps say some are platform-specific like p2exe for Windows and py2app for MacOS - although it would be fair to add that py2exe is Python 2 only and is not currently being maintained. I believe py2app is active but I haven't personally used it with Python 3. Others like cx-freeze and pyinstaller are cross-platform, although the build for a specific OS needs to be done under that OS - possibly in a virtual machine running in a different OS. Both cx-freeze and pyinstaller are currently active and being maintained. All of the above are available through PyPI and pip.

The documentation for pyinstaller is at https://pyinstaller.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ and the first time you run it for ``main.py`` it creates a setup file called ``main.spec`` and if anything non-standard is required or there are hidden imports to be found, the .spec file can be modified and future builds run against that instead of the .py file. Was the example in the docs here? I would like to update the code.

So,when you trigger an "updateAll" it calls the remove function of **all** installed widgets, not just the widgets listed in the [`widgets` option](http://mottie.github.io/tablesorter/docs/#widgets).

Because of the possibility of columns being removed or added, the filter widget removes the filter row and rebuilds it. I know you're not using the filter widget, but since the second row in the header has a "tablesorter-filter-row" class name, it is being removed. The filter widget also makes all rows visible upon removal.

So you have two choices:

1. Remove the "tablesorter-filter-row" class name, and instead of using `display:none` on the rows, add a class name, something like `hidden`, which has a defined `display: none` value.
2. Only load the widgets being used, like the stickyHeaders widget (zebra is part of the core) instead of using the `jquery.tablesorter.widgets.js` file. Or, you could create a [custom combined file](https://github.com/Mottie/tablesorter/wiki/Customize) that doesn't include the filter widget.

Rambling thoughts to self: This functionality may actually be conceptually incompatible with how Releases currently works!

----

Consider the following scenario, where a project was on a stable 1.5 line, gets some bugfixes, adds some new backwards compatible features for release as 1.6, and also adds some backwards *incompatible* features for release as 2.0.

They then have a release day, putting out all 3 releases (1.5 bugfix, 1.6 feature, 2.0 backwards incompat feature):

``` rst
* :release:`2.0.0 <2016-04-25>`
* :release:`1.6.0 <2016-04-25>`
* :release:`1.5.1 <2016-04-25>`
* :feature:`123` Super big huge backwards incompatible feature!
* :feature:`99` Regular feature!
* :bug:`101` Oops, a regular ol' bug.
* :release:`1.5.0 <2016-01-25>`
<prehistory here>
```

What should happen in this case, re: which "buckets" each item goes into? Currently we do roughly the following:

* Start with just two buckets, `unreleased_bugfix` and `unreleased_feature`
* As we walk up the changelog from the beginning of time, `:release:` roles cause new per-line buckets to be added, going by major+minor version, so `:release:1.0.0` generates a `1.0` line bucket, same for `1.1`, etc.
* When non-release items are encountered, they get put into all applicable buckets: bugfix items go into every release line + `unreleased_bugfix`, feature/support go only into `unreleased_feature`, etc. (With appropriate exceptions for major bugs or backported feature/support items.)
* Encountering bugfix `:release` roles (defined simply as "releases whose line has already been seen") causes their lines' buckets to get emptied out, so if we've been walking up the timeline after `1.0.0` - consuming bugfixes into a `1.0` bucket - encountering `1.0.1` causes us to dump the contents of `1.0` into the `1.0.1` release.
* Encountering feature `:release:` roles (defined as "releases whose line we haven't seen before") just empty out `unreleased_features`.

So, given the above changelog, again ignoring `2.0.0`, you'd end up with:

* `1.5.1`: bug 101
* `1.6.0`: features 123, 99

Great. So what happens when we *do* want to consider 2.0?

----

In a super naive setup, where major releases follow from minor ones *but not at the same time* (i.e. if we dropped `1.6.0` from the example) things appear to "work" - but only insofar as it's `2.0.0` that gets features 123 and 99.

Conversely, what happens in the full example is that `1.6.0` "eats" both features out of `unreleased_features`, leaving it empty when `2.0.0` is encountered. Whoops! (And empty releases don't really count, so they don't get displayed. Bit of a corner case...)

Now, some (maybe even most) projects *may* happen to fit this scenario, but there's nothing about good versioning practices that says you can't continue putting out 1.x feature releases after 2.0.0.

Additionally, there's the issue of bugs: in a 1.x-and-2.x world, where 2.x is backwards incompatible from 1.x (potentially even a full rewrite!) you can't naively assume all bugs encountered recently belong in both e.g. 1.5.3 and 2.0.1. (Frustratingly, of course, you *also* can't assume *none* of them do, either - only in a full-rewrite scenario is that likely the case.)

So we clearly need either some serious annotation (if we continue "one timeline rules them all") or consider reorganization somehow (perhaps multiple changelog files and/or sections).

Annotation seems like it'd get annoying fast: requiring e.g. everything belonging only to 2.0+ to get tagged with "2.0" or "2" or whatever. But it allows one to continue the existing "bend the rules" feature set of major bugs and backported non-bugs; it means one can continue a single timeline without duplicating anything. Clearly best suited for a world where 2.0, 3.0 etc aren't hugely divergent from 1.x or each other.

Splitting into multiple sections or files (doesn't matter which, I don't think; either way we'd just be merging them together in the end) allows for easier separation, but means the entire raison d'etre of this library is nullified: now if you have a bug affecting 1.x and 2.x (or, even 1.x, 2.x and 3.x...etc) it must be copied into each section. For libraries where the major revisions *aren't* full-on rewrites, this is real bad. For libraries that *are*, it's less bad - but maybe in that case we just want to make it easy to generate multiple, completely distinct changelog files (as in, ones which might even render to separate target files)?

I could't find any delete either. I ended up using a REGEX after querying the entire set of records.
So basically, query the entire set of records, check for the entry matching the one you want to delete, then if found use preg_replace to set that entry to blank (removing it) and PUT the date back.

Since I wanted the entire record (every record) for a given host name I used this REGEX:

$regex="/{[^{]+\"".$hostname."\"[^}]+},{0,1}/i";

Then I used that in preg_match to see if it exists and if it does then I use the same regex in preg_replace with a PUT to delete it. 

Clearly, if you wanted a specific type of record you could just add that to the regex string.

The problem is *preloading*, because the entire dataset is first loaded in memory. Execution doesn't take place until then.

In order to keep the project as *pure* Python as possible the internal memory storage buffer is `array.array`and the problem being experienced here is the continuous *reallocation*.

The options are:

  - Disable `preload` in `cerebro`

     `cerebro = bt.Cerebro(preload=False)` or `cerebro.run(preload=False)`

  - Use `exactbars` to decrease memory usage (and automatically disable `preload`)

     The docs: http://backtrader.readthedocs.io/en/latest/cerebro.html

     If you have no interest in *plotting* I would recommend `exactbars=0`

     This reduces all buffers to the bare minimum and removes any memory *reallocation* problems you may experience.

I just wanted to give you a heads up on the process of porting from cs:

The main container object has been broken apart so that all the tables variables are now at the top level.

Most init function are initializing the variable outside of their scope, I chose to not change the body of these functions except that they return the initialized table. They are called at the top level to initialize the top-level variables.

Most of the code has been ported by:
 * remove type information
 * for loops are pythonized by remove the index and looping directly on the table elements. To do so, the lists are cut to the right size. Initialization loops to invalid values are pythonized in list comprehensions.
 * Transform procedures that change global variables in place to functions that return the changed data. Obviously, the call sites must be changed too.

I expect to have push after and open a pull request after this first pass. The code will be all correct python code but presumably not working. This will be only for review. 

I haven't felt the need to use numpy to replace the loops. That may come in a second pass.

Any remark is welcome.

You'll still need the secret words though (to protect against network adversaries, or the server being compromised), so the code would be at least `wormhole receive AzS9sF-purple-sausages`. And we'd have to either require the server to run on a well-known port, or include the port number too (`wormhole receive AzS9Sf:4000-purple-sausages`). And I'd like this protocol to survive the transition to IPv6 (`wormhole receive 2600:3c01::e8f3:9145:fed4:59ed:4000-purple-sausages`), which makes the server addresses much to long to feasibly transcribe.

Also, base62 is maximal for things that need to be typed, but since the codes need to be verbally transcribable too, I don't think we can afford to have upper-vs-lowercase. So base32 or base36 is probably the densest we can do, which makes the encoded IP addresses slightly larger. A big part of the value of magic-wormhole is how easy the codes are to speak and to write, which constrains what we can do with the server address.

I'm currently just using inquirer with a custom command line app we use for managing aws cloudformation stacks. Unfortunately, it's not open source, so I can't add a reference to it in the readme, but FWIW, I've used inquirer to replace custom code to prompt for user input when the app is run in interactive mode (i.e. without all required options provided as command line arguments). We're prompting for input, confirming actions, asking users to select an option from a list, and asking users to select multiple options from a list using checkboxes.

I'm not really a Python programmer, having come mostly from the ruby world where I've used the tty gem for this kind of thing in the past. Inquirer is the nearest thing I've found for python, and so far is serving us very well.

Actually, come to think of it, another thing that struck me as odd and I quickly worked around was the way default values work when prompting for input.... Inquirer just prefills the field with the default, and users have to actually delete that value in order to override the default. I expected the default value to be shown in brackets or something like that, and if the user hit enter without typing anything that would mean accept the default, but if they wanted to override the default, they'd just type whatever value they wanted, without haing to delete the default value first. Here's some (horrible, because it's backwards compatible) code:

```
def prompt(message, default=None):
    # Inquirer's mechanism for handling defaults is a bit weird. It prefills
    # the text entry rather than dealing with users entering no value. Users
    # then need to delete the default value in order to enter a custom value.
    # So, we'll explicitly handle defaults so they work more conventionally.
    if default is not None:
        message = message + ' [{}]'.format(default)

    questions = [
        inquirer.Text(
            'text',
            message=message
        )
    ]
    answers = inquirer.prompt(questions)
    if answers is None:
        raise KeyboardInterrupt

    value = answers['text']
    if value == '' and default is not None:
        return str(default) # always return a string
    else:
        return value
```

In general I try to avoid having the parser correct mistakes in the input, just because there are so many potential mistakes and correcting one frequently causes other valid input to not work. It's more important that it work correctly for input with no mistakes. But it would be nice if the parser could be useful a useful tool for that because the reality is that these mistakes sometimes exist in the input.

One approach would be to use the `preprocess()` method to do some regex replacing on the whole string before it is parsed to correct the mistake, whenever you find some variation of "ph. d." replace it with "ph.d." or something. That would be fairly simple, you could do it pretty easily with subclassing HumanName and little understanding of the class' inner workings. But that would actually change the string so what you got back would not equal what you input. Some people don't like that.

Another approach would be to make the parser recognize "Ph d" as a suffix. This would be somewhat difficult because at the moment the first thing the parser does is break up the string on spaces, so "ph" and "d" are in different pieces. Maybe you could do something like with the conjunctions, whenever you find a "ph" by itself connect it to the following piece, i guess only if it's a "d". But it's hard to imagine an agnostic solution that would be helpful for more than just "ph d". Can you think of other similar examples?

I feel like ideally I'd like to have the parser do something to make it easy for each developer to handle correcting the input for their particular use case. Not sure the best way to do that though, partly because I know so little about how people actually use this parser. Suggestions welcome.

The memory examples use a recurrent network (`neat.nn.RecurrentNetwork`), which remembers the node outputs from the last time it was run.  Also, `feed_forward` is set to `False` in the DefaultGenome section of the config, and this allows the networks to make arbitrary connections between nodes, instead of just having forward connections.  

So when you run  the network with the second set of inputs, the final node values can be a mixture of the previous run's values, and the current values.  This is what's different from the network in examples like XOR (`neat.nn.FeedForwardNetwork`); for those networks, each time you run it with new inputs, all the node values are replaced with values computed only from the current inputs.

It depends on a couple things:
1. What is the size of your "cluster"?  If it's a small number that you are managing yourself, you might want to just start with distributed tensorflow, so you will not need to install/learn Spark, and you can get started by following their examples fairly closely.  If it's a large cluster, or you will not be managing the cluster, then you would need some base layer of distributed filesystem and node management.  TensorFlow uses Kubernetes.  But for folks already on Hadoop/YARN/Spark, TFoS is a simple way to use distributed TF.
2. If you want to use Keras (separately from the `tf.keras` API in TF), then you'd likely need to install various dependencies (which implies that you will be managing these nodes yourself).  See #1.
3. What is the size of your data?  If you can fit your data on a single node by copying files around, then you MIGHT be able to get by without using a distributed filesystem.  But if you have more than is practical to fit on a single disk (or copy around), then you will definitely need some form of a distributed filesystem.
4. Do you have other pre-processing or post-processing tasks that you'd want to use Spark for?  If so, you'd want Spark w/ a distributed filesystem anyways.

Anyhow, those are the major points... hope that helps.

@dann815 There are certain User-specific actions allowed by the API that the SDK doesn't explicitly support:

- email aliases
- (admin only) move user's folder, change user properties, roll user out of enterprise
- change login email

Right now, any of these are possible by using ``client.make_request`` with the correct HTTP verb, URL, and parameters, but it would be a lot easier if the SDK supported these actions natively.

When you go through the /authorize workflow, it is best practice for your application to insert a randomly generated token as the ``state`` parameter. https://box-content.readme.io/reference#authorize

Then, after the redirect occurs, before calling ``authenticate()`` with the received auth code, you should assert that the ``state`` param you received in the payload of the redirect request matches the expected ``state`` value that you had passed. This is a way to assure you that the response was not forged.

Check out https://github.com/box/box-python-sdk/blob/634b6d6a85de85f3f4076d6cf8d5883053c971da/demo/auth.py#L57 for an example of this.

See also http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-10.12 for more information.

If you are handling the redirect in the same Python session that made the authorize call, then you can just check the value of ``csrf_token`` that you had generated earlier in the session, as is done in our demo that I linked to.

If you're making a server-based application, then you'll need some way to store the csrf token you generated, and look it up when you receive a redirect.

@lanking520 The code you pointed out is correct, it disposes the NDArrays in the DataBatch created using the Slice operator. Slice operator does not create new NDArrays instead gives a reference to the original NDArray with an offset. The pointers to the NDArrays(both sliced and original) use shared_ptr which only decrements the reference count, so it won't free the original NDArray correspondingly if you don't dispose the Sliced NDArray it won't free the original even if the original NDArray is freed.

Each upsample layer consists of (1) Upsample (bilinear, zero padding, nearest- neighbor) and (2) convolution with a transposed filter. That is why you need the convolution operation in these layers. Convolution always reduce the input matrix to the output, to go to a bigger output, you have to upsample the input first and potentially do zero - padding to the output later on in order to fix the dimensions. Also, the filters are called learned filters, because the un-transposed version (filter) that we used to calculate the transposed filter learned the features during training.

Eventually I did it... 

```
all: zeromq
zeromq: zeromq-4.1.3.tar.gz
	@echo -e '\e[1;32mInstalling $(strip $<)\e[0m' 
	if [ ! -f /usr/lib/libzmq.dll.a ]; then \
		tar xvzf $<; \
		cd $(patsubst %.tar.gz,%, $<); \
		./configure; \
	    patch src/curve_client.cpp ../curve_client.patch; \
	    patch src/curve_server.cpp ../curve_server.patch; \
	    make -j4; \
		make install; \
		gcc -shared -o cygzmq.dll -Wl,--out-implib=libzmq.dll.a -Wl,--export-all-symbols -Wl,--enable-auto-import -Wl,--whole-archive .libs/libzmq.a -Wl,--no-whole-archive -lstdc++ -L/usr/local/lib -lsodium; \
		cp src/libzmq.pc /usr/lib/pkgconfig/; \
		cp include/zmq.h /usr/include/; \
		cp include/zmq_utils.h /usr/include/; \
		cp cygzmq.dll /usr/bin/; \
		cp libzmq.dll.a /usr/lib/; \
		cd .. && rm -rf $(patsubst %.tar.gz,%, $<); \
	else \
		echo -e '   \e[1;33mDone\e[0m\n'; \
	fi

all: pyzmq
pyzmq: pyzmq-14.7.0.tar.gz
	@echo -e '\e[1;32mInstalling $(strip $2)\e[0m' 
	if ! python -c 'import $@'; then \
		cd $(patsubst %.tar.gz,%, $<); \
		python setup.py build_ext --zmq=/usr; \
		python setup.py test --zmq=/usr; \
		python setup.py install --zmq=/usr; \
		cd .. && rm -rf $(patsubst %.tar.gz,%, $<); \
	else \
		echo -e '   \e[1;33mDone\e[0m\n'; \
	fi	   
```

just realized that `enable_distributed` needs to be supported on a per-session because otherwise it will mess up tracing with aiobotocore as it would add headers to AWS calls breaking them.

1) We do use django-channels for websockets which uses redis as the cache layer. So probably that could be a reason as it uses the same django settings. And that's why encapsulating ddtrace-run over uwsgi or gunicorn or celery workers would make more sense than configuring int the settings.py as it will be shared by many other apps/services.

2) Yes, they are made from `requests` lib. Not even a single HTTP request is being traced as a metric across thousands of requests logged. Probably it is not patched when configuring through settings.py.

3) We would prefer to use ddtrace-run as we can control what needs to be traced under a particular service. For example, I would like to see uwsgi and celery as different services in the dashboard. And saying so, when using uwsgi/gunicorn along with `--gevent` or any other event loop and `--processes`, anyone would prefer to measure the concurrency and see the traces per process if possible.   ```
  ```
GIT_DIR
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE
GIT_EDITOR
GIT_INDEX_FILE
GIT_AUTHOR_NAME
GIT_PREFIX
GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL
```

- the *gitpython* library sets only 2 env-vars: 

  ```
GIT_INDEX_FILE
GIT_EDITOR`
```

```python
    pipenv run flake8 dag
```

@drag1flye regarding your first few questions, I don't think there is a memory leak in `rgb2grey`. To my knowledge, it is a pure python function which simply adds things together.

Transform resize calls `scipy.ndimage.guassianfilter` so if there is a memory leak, it might be in there.
But to actually prove you had a memory leak in the function, you would have to show that this function crashes your computer:

```python
import numpy as np
from skimage.transform import resize
img = np.zeros((1000, 1000))
for i in range(10000000):
     resize(img, (100, 100))
```

In this example, you are just analysing a single image, and that would indicate that everytime you call the function, a little bit more memory is allocated.


That said, your example doesn't show the memory leak as much as you are hitting your memory limit of your super computer. This is quite common!
This is because:
1. You are loading ALL your images
2. You convert ALL the images to grey
3. At the end of the script, both the original images and the grey images are in memory.

I would suggest creating a function, that looks like

```python
def load_image_as_grey_same_size(filename):
    image = skimage.io.imread(filename)
    image = transform.resize(image, (400, 400))
    image = rgb2gray(image)  # in 0.13, this always returns a 64bit float
    image = img_as_float32(image)  # Optional: set to your desired precision
    return image
```
that way, when you load your images, only one full image is kept in memory at a time. 
Your images are quickly resized, and the full image is discarded without creating a giant list of them.


PS. The correct place to import imread from is `skimage.io` and not `skimage.data`. `data` is more for the `skimage` supplied data.

FYI, I added a workaround myself like below so that I keep my Celery LOGLEVEL at INFO while sending WARNING to Sentry.

```
        # TODO: Drop this workaround after bugfix of Raven 5.6.0.
        # Ref - https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/issues/649
        class WarningFilter(logging.Filter):
            def filter(self, record):
                return record.levelno >= logging.WARNING

        def sentry_logging_filter(sender, logger, loglevel, logfile, format,
                                  colorize, **kw):
            for h in logger.handlers:
                if type(h) == SentryHandler:
                    filter_ = WarningFilter()
                    h.addFilter(filter_)
                    return False
        after_setup_logger.connect(sentry_logging_filter, weak=False)
        # End workaround for bug in Raven 5.6.0
```

@mmalohlava 
If I understand you correctly, you are suggesting the following mechanism:
* Whenever a VisServer needs to aggregate a data frame (which exists as the name of a file on disk), it launches a new system process invoking the python interpreter.
* You pass that process via STDIN a python program that goes approximately like this:
```
import datatable as dt
from datatable.extras.autovis import aggregate
df = dt.open(name_of_dataset)
for params in ...:
    dfagg = aggregate(df, params)
    dfagg.save(params.name_of_output_dataset)
```
* When the process finishes, you will have a certain number of files created on the file system, which will be then loaded into Java.
* If VizServer wants to do things in parallel, it will launch multiple python processes instead, and distribute the workload among those processes manually.
* VizServer will ensure that the python is launched within the correct virtual environment (with datatable installed), as well as handle all error messages that may be returned from python.

just so I have this right, because I think I have a version of doing this that I could add to galsim:

if the wavefront W(rho, phi) = sum_i a_i Z_i(rho, phi), you want:

dW/dx (rho, phi) = sum_i b^x_i Z_i(rho, phi) (and dW/dy, too)

where b_i = gamma^x_{ij} a_j (ie a matrix multiplication).

I also have code that takes a_i Z_i * a_j Z_j = sum_k b_k Z_k (ie zernike multiplication), which I have found is super useful if you need to compute integrals of products of zernikes.

Only reason that this *seems* to work for you, is that including the font-face definition in an earlier location in your CSS in **»this one specific occasion«** only, causes the font to finish loading before IE's first crack at rendering the page.

Thus you avoid the issue with IE not repainting pseudo-elements, because they're already completely rendered as they should be *on first paint*.

However, you have absolutely zero control over all the timing conditions involved and thus **zero** guarantee that this will work correctly everywhere on all connections for everyone.

Here's how it works according to my best knowledge. I might easily be wrong, though.

In Debian, packages make it to experimental first, then unstable, then testing, then stable. I have no clue how much it takes from experimental to unstable. As far as I recall, from unstable to testing is pretty much automatic and happens in about a week unless there are known troubles with the package.

In Ubuntu, most packages (probably Guake too) are set to automatically be synced from Debian testing. For Bionic, this auto import will [stop on Mar 1](https://wiki.ubuntu.com/BionicBeaver/ReleaseSchedule). After this point it's still possible to get an update, but requires a contact person who's happy to work on it.

So, ideally, the safest path is to make sure it arrives at Debian testing way before Mar 1, which probably means unstable before Feb twenty-few, which mean experimental I-don't-know-when. I guess this process should be started now with the beta release (as far as I understand Daniel already took care of it), and then hopefully there'll be time for another one (or maybe even two) updates based on feedback, in time for Bionic.

Before writing modified (dirty) data to a primary file, a hive writer will store this data in a transaction log file. If an error (like a system crash) occurs when writing to a transaction log file, a primary file will remain consistent; if an error occurs when writing to a primary file, a transaction log file will contain enough data to recover a primary file and bring it back to the consistent state.

Imageio makes use of freeimage for reading/writing png data. Good to hear it supports reading in this format. I am not sure whether it supports writing to it, but we can try.

This would need some changes in the imageio code, because currently, in the png writer, we explicitly convert to uint8 (https://github.com/imageio/imageio/blob/master/imageio/plugins/freeimage.py#L225).

I don't have an awful lot of time to look into it, but I am happy to guide you in trying to make it work.
* In the above link, you should remove the conversion to uint8
* The code then moves to `Writer._append_data()`, which makes calls into the freeimage wrapper here https://github.com/imageio/imageio/blob/master/imageio/plugins/freeimage.py#L126-L128
* Allocate: https://github.com/imageio/imageio/blob/master/imageio/plugins/freeimage.py#L126-L128
* set_image_data: https://github.com/imageio/imageio/blob/master/imageio/plugins/_freeimage.py#L938
* I am not sure how well our freeimage wrapper deals with 16bit data. Maybe it just works, or maybe it needs some changes ...

> rfcf.compact = True;

That's an outdated "option configuration" syntax. You should be using `PMMLPipeline.configure(**pmml_options)` now.

The `compact = True` option, when actually applied, should decrease the size of the PMML file around 50%. Export the same pipeline first with `compact = False` and then with `compact = True`.

Also, please note the this 2.7 GB PMML file probably contains 2 GB of "XML markup" and 0.7 GB of whitespace (in the form of tab characters). You may safely strip the latter.

Anyway, the size of the PMML file in local filesystem is quite irrelevant. What really needs to be optimized is RAM consumption when it is parsed/deployed in the production system.

@jeffbaumes I think your assessment is correct.  As @cjhi1 points out,  both the standard worker specification and generic celery tasks could benefit from using signals to update job metadata.

signals work like hooks and allow arbitrary functions to be run on
+ before_task_publish
+ after_task_publish
+ task_prerun
+ task_postrun
+ task_retry
+ task_success
+ task_failure
+ task_revoked

they are defined independent of individual tasks and so could apply across the board to spec based tasks, as well as custom tasks. e.g.

```python
import requests
from celery.signals import after_task_publish

@after_task_publish.connect
def task_sent_handler(sender=None, body=None, **kwargs):
    jobInfo = kwargs['headers']['jobInfo']
    requests.patch("jobs/%s/status" % jobInfo['_id'], 
                   headers={'Girder-Token': jobInfo['token']},
                   data= {'status': 'published'})
```

The `autobahn.wamp.cryptosign._makepad` function doesn't work properly in Python 3.

`_makepad(6)` returns `'\x01\x02\x03\x04\x05\x06'` (a str) rather than `b'\x01\x02\x03\x04\x05\x06'` (bytes). The str/bytes type difference causes the `pad != _makepad(len(pad))` the statement to fail.

My workaround is to monkey patch it: 

```
import autobahn.wamp.cryptosign
def _makepad_py3(size):
    return bytes(range(1, size + 1))
autobahn.wamp.cryptosign._makepad = _makepad_py3
```

This solution unfortunately does not work in Python2. To make it compatible with both, you'd need to do something like this: 
```
def _makepad_py23(size): 
    ''.join([chr(x) for x in range(1, size + 1)]).encode('ascii')
```
(I would hope there is a cleaner-looking solution, but that seems to work.)

Ok, there are 2 things here: the concrete issue, and the general issue ("public API").

The constructor has changed: https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/wamp/types.py#L482

```python
def __init__(self, subscription, publication, publisher=None, publisher_authid=None, publisher_authrole=None, topic=None, retained=None, enc_algo=None):
```

Likely, your test will work if doing `EventDetails(None, 1, topic=topic)`.

---

Rgd "public API": the _class_ itself is [marked public](https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/wamp/types.py#L481), but [not the constructor](https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/wamp/types.py#L499).

This marking is intentional: all the properties of `EventDetails` are considered public, but NOT the ctor (there is no user need to construct it).

However we fail in two aspects here:

1. The [docs](https://autobahn.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/autobahn.wamp.html#autobahn.wamp.types.EventDetails) do not reflect this properly: the documentation makes no distinction between instance attributes being consider public (as is the case of all of `wamp.types.*`  classes) versus the constructor method itself being public or not
2. There are classes in `wamp.types.*` where the constructor itself must be public, eg `SubscribeOptions` - that is, all classes supposed to be instantiated by the user.

---

To sum up, I take respons. for breaking stuff (sorry:( - and I would like to improve on that;) However, how exactly .. see above .. needs discussions.

What I would like to avoid is a general "every change must work with existing code" policy. This is holding back refactorings, making things more ugly etc.

So I would like to have a crystal clear way of communicating what is public and what is not, and only for the former adhere to a strict policy of not breaking code.

## Split API in two

I like the idea of splitting this into two APIs:

- `w3.eth.overrideTransaction(txn_hash, new_transaction_dict)` -- Use only the new transaction fields, do not reuse any values from the the current pending transaction
- `w3.eth.modifyTransaction(txn_hash, **individual_fields_to_replace)` -- Use the keyword args provided, infer `gasPrice` if not provided, and then fill in unspecified values from the current pending transaction

Examples:

```py
from web3.auto import w3

txn_b = {'from': w3.eth.accounts[0], 'to': w3.eth.accounts[1], value: 0, gas: 21000}
txn_b_hash = w3.eth.overrideTransaction(txn_a_hash, txn_b)
```

```py
new_txn_hash = w3.eth.modifyTransaction(txn_hash, gasPrice=w3.toWei('5.5', 'gwei'))
```

## Gas price inference

> if `gasPrice` is not included in the `txn_overrides` we should ???? (TODO: how do we choose an appropriate gas price?).

Proposal -- get two values, and use the maximum price of the two:

- The gas price of the pending transaction times 1.1 (the minimum required to propagate)
- The gas price as calculated by the current gas price engine

I have no idea why your `vim` is trying to load Qt (that is, AFAIK, the only thing that links to libselinux; it does this because QtWebkit uses JIT and for systems that *are* running SELinux, this is essential in order to mark heap pages as being executable). You might want to verify what's being loaded here by using `strace`, or perhaps `ldd vim`.

Regardless, to run Qt from Conda libselinux is a system requirement and for `ArchLinux` that requirement is satisfied via `sudo pacman -S libselinux`.

@dtrizna sorry for the late response. 

It's not very clear, and I apologize for that, but the `default_operator` only works for `query_string` queries. What that means is you need to use the `q` parameter as well in your `search()`. 

```
search = es.search( index='my_index', _source=fields, request_timeout=600, size=5000, default_operator='AND', q='some query string', sort=['@timestamp'], body={ "query": { "bool": { "filter": { "term": { "field": "value" } }, "must_not": { "term": { "field": "value", "field": "value2" } } } } } )
```

Documentation for [query string queries](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/query-dsl-query-string-query.html)

If the PM is looking at this, Dear PM - this is the possible root cause: There is a bug with sysprep when it's done multiple times on the same image.

I follow this process:

1. Create a VM from any Windows Server image.
2. Sysprep and Capture Image as 'img01'
3. Create new VM from Image 'img01'.
4. Sysprep and Capture again - repeat this process 2 more times.

Any VM created this way will retain the same hostname.

Why would I do such a crazy thing you ask? Because when I capture image, the original VM is destroyed, so I want to keep making changes to whatever VMs I have (these VMs could have undergone the capture process multiple times).

I just verified requests does NOT close the stream as I would have expected.   If someone creates the stream they are explicit owners of said stream and should handle closing as they are the only ones that know when it should be closed.  Only if they tell aiohttp to close it should it be closed.  Otherwise aiohttp cannot know if the caller wants to use the stream again after the request.

```
 ------------------------ ------------ --------------------------------------------

 ------------------------ ------------ --------------------------------------------
| field                  | on_h_value | Value for current header started. Allocate |

 ------------------------ ------------ --------------------------------------------

 ------------------------ ------------ --------------------------------------------
```

I think I have a clue:

When I remove the `'user': 'instructor1',` line from the service config, it does this:

* It finds the config file `/home/instructor1/nbgrader/course1`.
* It creates `gradebook.db` and `.nbgrader.log` as root, and with permissions that don't allow `instructor1` to access them.

From this I can conclude:

* JupyterHub doesn't properly set the `cwd` if the `user` option is used.
* The `user` option is needed to set permissions properly for nbgrader usage.

Here is an issue on jupyterhub that tracks the issue on that side: https://github.com/jupyterhub/jupyterhub/issues/935

@terrajobst - Hi,

OK I guess this is dead in the water as a realistic expectation for future support. But I would argue that supporting this for non-partial types is better than not supporting it at all. 

After all saying "Ordering a type's reflected members by MetadataToken is guaranteed to order members in their declared order but only if the type is not a partial type." is rather a useful thing to be able to say. Like any feature there are limits on its flexibility.

This isn't a feature that's going to be used by every developer every day but the ability to get things in a predictable order like this is hugely useful when one does require it. 

If you look at the exposed properties of the [PropertyInfo ](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/system.reflection.propertyinfo(v=vs.110).aspx)class from .Net 1.1 to today, there are quite a few properties that have been added over the years (e.g. [CustomAttributes](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/system.reflection.memberinfo.customattributes(v=vs.110).aspx), [ReflectedType](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/system.reflection.memberinfo.reflectedtype(v=vs.110).aspx)) old contracts are honored but those using new contracts get the new features - so we could in principle see a new property on a Type named (say) "SourceOffset" a long if the MetadataToken is too challenging as a way to do this.

All this amounts to is _implicit metadata_, as opposed to explicit metadata (attributes etc).  

As for the promise that would be simply what I outlined above - no guarantee is made for partial classes - simple as that, just like renaming a property in some class is not guaranteed to have no impact on XAML consumers of that class or other mechanisms that use reflection on that class.

I cannot say just how much effort is involved or what the pros/cons are for this - I am suggesting it and if it really is too hard to do then that's that !

Thanks for discussing this anyway, I'll consider this well and truly closed.

@kuba 
> For longer hashes please consider that some filesystems might have character limits for filenames

MD5 creates 32 hexadecimal character long filenames and if we assume SHA-256 is safe enough, we would end up with a 64 character long hexadecimal file name. If we look at a [list of file system limits](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Comparison_of_file_systems#Limits), we can conclude the ONLY two file systems affected by this change would be:

* FATX;
* Minix V3 FS.

All other file systems in that list either wouldn't fit MD5 anyway ór would fit a SHA-256 hash.

So unless someone is trying to install Let's Encrypt on a XBox or a system with the Minix V3 file system (never heard of it :stuck_out_tongue:), you're good to go with SHA-256.

Seriously, put more effort into it when reporting an issue!

- Please post minimal examples!! I can't overstate the importance of this. Your posted files contain lots of code which is completely unrelated to the problem. Often, when creating minimal examples, you will figure out the problem on your own.
- The issue title is indescriptive.
- The premise of your first question is wrong, as the output shows (`++++++ warning: unknown sequence skipped: x`; `++++++ warning: unknown sequence ignored: x`)
- Don't post anything that requires me clicking (annoying madx plots), except when it is essential to the problem.
- State the file names of the posted code. Otherwise, I will have to read your code before executing it in order to just figure out the file names. This is especially annoying since you didn't post minimal examples.
- (About your previous post:) You asked me how to plot multiple lines in matplotlib - don't ask me questions which you will find the answer in 1 minute searching on the internet!


**Question 1**

Short version: It doesn't work. Always make sure to use the correct sequence name! Read the MAD-X output carefully, it tells you that it doesn't work.

If you had created a minimal example you would have noticed this on your own (as soon as you remove the TWISS command from your madx file)!

Explanation: There is a TWISS command in your .madx file. MAD-X then stores the calculated values in a memory table named "twiss". When executing `madx.twiss(sequence='x')` from your python code, cpymad executes a command `TWISS, sequence=x, ...`. For some reason MAD-X thinks that this is not an error and ignores the attempt. It does not signal the error so there is no way to infer this from python, and so the the python code just continues. It next loads values from the "twiss" table assuming its filled with happy new values. Since you previously calculated TWISS in some other way, there are indeed values, so this doesn't crash either. But it *is an error*!

**Question 2**

1. you can use the `range` parameter of TWISS/`madx.twiss()`. But it may not work when multiple elements have the same name.
2. You can slice the array manually after figuring out what the index is. This looks like the following:

```
twiss = madx.twiss(...)
elements = madx.sequences['cassps'].elements
start = elements.index('qd:10')
stop = elements.index('qd:16')
twiss = {twiss[k][start:stop] for k in ['s', 'betx', 'bety']}
plt.plot(twiss['s'], twiss['betx'])
...
```

And by the way: why do you intermix the native madx twiss+plotting and cpymad? The whole purpose of cpymad is to access the madx in data in python, so you can use more sophisticated tools for data analysis, such as matplotlib.

### First
For `senza scale` last arguments is always a number of instances to scale up/down. In you case `0460` but there is a limit to 100 instances. That's why it was scaled only to 100 instances. It does not matter what number will be. If it > 100 then it will be 100.

`senza scale hecate-live.yml 0460` is a valid command because stack can have no version at all.
Also available commands are:
* `senza scale hecate-live.yml v10123sdfs 0460` 
Scale stack `<stack_name>-v10123sdfs` to 100 instances. Instead of `<stack_name>` will be value from `hecate-live.yml` from attribute `SenzaInfo/StackName`.
* `senza scale stack1 v1 stack2 v2 10`
Scale two stacks `stack1-v1` and `stack2-v2` to 10 instances.

> I agree, not the most useful use cases. But it is one.

Thanks for chiming in! However, what we meant by `train()` was not a Theano function, but a higher-level function encapsulating part of a training loop. The question was whether we'd do an API like the following, with a stateful function:
```python
for loss in train(num_epochs, all_the_other_arguments):
    ...
```
or something like this, with a stateless function:
```python
for _ in range(num_epochs):
    loss = train(all_the_other_arguments)
    ...
```
My argument was that the latter is a little more flexible and transparent since you control all of the loop, but it only works if `train()` does not need to keep any state between calls. Thus we're trying to find use cases for a stateful training loop function, to see if it's needed.

Thanks for the report! Unfortunately, I'm not really sure if and what to do about it -- in the case of MNIST digits, replacing out-of-sample pixels with zero is an obvious solution because the background is black, for other images this may not be suitable at all. We could add a border mode argument to the layer to specify what should happen with out-of-sample pixels (clip to border as we do now, fill with a given constant, mirror the input, or repeat the input). `scipy.ndimage` has this for many of its filters:
```
mode : {'reflect', 'constant', 'nearest', 'mirror', 'wrap'}, optional
    The `mode` parameter determines how the array borders are
    handled, where `cval` is the value when mode is equal to
    'constant'. Default is 'reflect'
cval : scalar, optional
    Value to fill past edges of input if `mode` is 'constant'. Default
    is 0.0
```
Mirroring and wrapping are trivial modifications of the current code (replacing `T.clip` with other operations), replacing by a constant is more involved. Maybe it's actually a good workaround to pad the image with a border of the value you'd like. In any case, a PR adding a `border_mode` argument or similarly would be welcome!

> I don't know if anyone is in favour of container classes?

I'm in favor of having both eventually -- something to make it easy to implement common step functions (such as LSTM, GRU), and something to create the `scan` call so you can include it in a standard Lasagne DAG of layers. I've listed some of the use cases we'd want it to solve at the end of https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/issues/425#issuecomment-157744032. I'd add "feeding back the output", as we'd like that for recurrent decoders.

If your "cells" can handle all those use cases with some custom `scan()` call on top, then they're probably a good basis and the container can be added any time later. We should be sure we're not missing any important use case, though, as we don't want to overhaul the recurrent layers a second time!

Happy to help!  I think setting pixels outside the grid to a constant value would be a useful border mode to have, but I'm not sure how that would be implemented.  Perhaps I'll get back to it in the future.

@WardLT and I talked about this is #119 I think. Double underscore prepends the class name (in this case the parent class `BaseFeaturizer`) to `__ignore_errors` with "name mangling". 

This would be useful in the case when `._ignore_errors` is some private attribute of a Featurizer, since `_ignore_errors` can be overridden by the child but `_BaseFeaturizer__ignore_errors` cannot. Outside of that use case, there is no reason for not using `_ignore_errors`.

This is one area where it would be better not to follow the example of the test module. The `test_initializer` class is convenient, but only useful when there are no dependencies between the different translation units (`cpp` files) as is the case for `pybind11_tests`. Each `test_initializer` is a global variable and C++ does not specify the initialization order of global variables in different translation units (the order is essentially random). So if your base and derived class wrappers are defined separately, there's a 50/50 chance that the derived wrapper will be initialized before the base, resulting in that error.

As a solution, use standard functions instead of the initializers, [as suggested in the docs](http://pybind11.readthedocs.io/en/latest/faq.html#how-can-i-reduce-the-build-time). It's a bit more typing, but it guarantees the initialization order.

This seems more like an intel compiler bug; declaring multiple variables with `auto` is definitely allowed by the standard as long as the variables are the same type (here both expressions are unequivocally `int`s).  I'm more tempted to just add a pragma to disable warning 3373 under icc to avoid the false positive warning.

@jagerman The SFINAE is required, but my main point is that it should be pushed down to `py::cast` or a new helper function so that it's only done in one place instead of having to repeat it for each interface function. This would simplify the accessor's `operator=` from 4 to 2 overloads and `append` from 2 to 1. Similarly new methods would only require 1.

`operator==` for pytypes just compares pointers -- it's equivalent to the `is` operator in Python (e.g. `obj1 is obj2`). But there can be two identical objects at different addresses which would return `false`. On the other hand, `isinstance<py::array_t<T>>` internally uses `PyArray_EquivTypes` which actually checks that the type objects are equivalent.

Nice. Python usually doesn't care about the specific sequence type so this makes a lot of sense. (This would also allow conversions of 1D `ndarray` to `std::vector`.)

IMHO It would be nice to have this for `std::tuple` as well. Although, there are 2 issues in that case, as far as I can see. I don't think either one is a showstopper, but some discussion/work is needed -- probably best to leave it for a separate PR. The two things are:

  1. As you mentioned, it would make overloading on `std::tuple` and `std::vector` ambiguous. But unless I'm missing something, I don't think this would be a problem in practice. First off, `std::vector` and `std::list` are already ambiguous overloads and it doesn't seem to have been an issue so far. Similarly, I don't think there's a lot of sense in exporting a function overloaded on `std::tuple` and `std::vector` to Python -- it's not very Pythonic to expect different results from `foo([1, 2, 3])` and `foo((1, 2, 3))`.

  2. The tuple caster also serves as the argument loader for function calls. This is pretty critical so it wouldn't be good to slow it down with the more generic sequence protocol. However, it may be nice to actually separate the argument loader from the tuple caster. That would make it possible to make the argument loader more specialized and faster (e.g. tuple and size checks are redundant since `dispatcher` already ensures this) and on the other hand make the tuple caster more versatile (accept sequences). I think this would be win for both.

The snippets in "Getting started" are made to fit commands on a single line, so to be copied easily into the interactive interpreter and allow going step-by-step without the need for larger function definitions. For a script I recommend to run the program logic in a new thread and have the in-built connect() loop do the housekeeping. That is something like this: http://pastebin.com/xfthYY5F where you can then press Ctrl-C to terminate earlier.

Thanks for the analysis, here are my thoughts:

- I'm not sure using yappi to analyze parallel execution time adds much here, since our code is sequential. It sends http requests in a loop and adds the results to the dataframe. There might be a point to be made here about making these requests in parallel, but I'm almost sure this isn't going to get us much, due to the next point.
- Using the table data list API is always going to be slower than having the service export the data to GCS and downloading it. We need to think about how we can do this in a transparent way, since we need an intermediate GCS bucket.
- In order to have a fair comparison, one step is missing in the analysis, which is loading the data from the downloaded blob into a dataframe, which the `to_dataframe` method takes care of as well.

@lukehoban   and i discussed this area quite a bit today.  We feel like there is likely room for improvements to the programming model here that will allow users to write code in the idiomatic style they expect, while also not opening up more cracks that lead to confusion.

The core thing we want to to solve is allowing users to be able to type ```import ... name ...``` at the top level, and then capture ```name``` inside 'cloud runtime functions' without hassle.  We think we can do that by re-introduce the concept of reifying captured modules as ```require```s inside captured functions. (note, there will be some tweaks here that will come lower)

Pros: 
1. Importing and using any *normal* JS module is trivial.  Users can use any idiomatic import style they want (i.e. ```outside import```, ```outside require```, ```inside await import```, ```inside require```), and it will all work.

Cons: 
1. Outside imports will 'resolve/execute' at deployment time, even if they are not needed at all during deployment.  This is likely not to actually be a problem.
2. Mutations to the module at deployment time will not be visible at runtime.  This may cause some confusion, but should be something we can easily explain to users.

Another concern we would have here *if we did nothing else* is that this would lead to a suboptimal experience around certain modules like ```@pulumi/pulumi```.  Namely, if we took the above approach **only**, then you would not be able to do ```new pulumi.Config("whatever")``` in your runtime code.  This is because that would translate into ```new require("pulumi").Config("whatever")```, which means your runtime code would never see the actual deployment time config values set whne we executed the pulumi-app.

To address that, we propose augment module serialization with the following capability:

1. Module objects can set a 'notAModule' bit on them, telling pulumi serialization to *not* treat a captured instance of as a module, and instead treat it like a normal value.  This will then cause us to have the same logic we have today, which means that 'Config' captures the current deployment time values for this object.

In essence this approach 'flips' how we treat modules today.  Today we say "all modules are values, and if you don't want that, don't even allow the module to go through closure serialization".  Instead, we'll have an approach that says "all modules are modules, and if you want a module to be captured by value, you mark it as such".

I personally think flipping things this way is appropriate.  In practice 99.99% of modules should not be treated as values.  So treating them as such, and basically failing 99.99% of hte time on them (since practically every module does something we cannot serialize) isn't a great default.

--

One concern we have here is that we have to be careful in terms of ensuring that the "reify as require" works properly for local-modules, and not just modules in the node_modules directory.

For example, if the user does: ```import * as x from "./x"```, we need to ensure that we are ```require```ing the right path.  Luke thinks this will actually work out thanks to how node module resolution works.  But we need to be careful we don't break this.

## Re: Timing data

Here's a simple script I used to run `pulumi preview` against the LM-cts stack to get a feel for the impact this change will have.

When ran against my devstack, this PR increases the time it takes to run `pulumi preview` from ~7s to ~11s. That isn't great, but since the stack has 160 resources I'm not sure the overhead is bad enough to consider design changes. e.g. if it is just an averaged ~25ms per resource to start with, and we can improve this as we land parallelism, compressing request payloads, etc.

/cc @lukehoban 

```bash
set -e -x

export AWS_REGION="us-west-2"
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID="AKI..."
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY="fr4..."
export PULUMI_API="https://api...."
export PULUMI_ACCESS_TOKEN="ea8..."

cd ${GOPATH}/src/github.com/pulumi/home/examples/learningmachine-cts/
pushd ./tool/infrastructure/pulumi > /dev/null
yarn
yarn run build
popd

export TIMESTAMP=$(date +%s)
pulumi login --cloud-url ${PULUMI_API}
pulumi stack init "lm-cts-preview-${TIMESTAMP}"

pulumi config set aws:region                  "${AWS_REGION}"
pulumi config set aws:accessKey               "${AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID}"
pulumi config set aws:secretKey               "${AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY}" --secret
pulumi config set cloud:provider              "aws"
pulumi config set cloud-aws:useFargate        "true"
pulumi config set cloud-aws:usePrivateNetwork "true"
... more config from the test ...

echo "AWS Identity"
aws sts get-caller-identity

export START_TIME=$(date)
pulumi preview
export END_TIME=$(date)

echo "Start: ${START_TIME}"
echo "End  : ${END_TIME}"

echo "Pulumi Version: $(pulumi version)"

echo "To cleanup the stack, run: pulumi stack rm "lm-cts-preview-${TIMESTAMP}" --yes"
```

After taking a deeper look at the PR, we must say again. Great Job guys!
It's good to see that ODC now has a proper plugin mechanism.

## Issues this PR solved for us:
* Proper plugin mechanism.
* Pickling metadata for load was failing on Python 2.
* Unnecessary database connections during data load.
* A learning exercise on how to better work in the ODC code base. To be discussed at the ODC Workshop.

**Note:** The list may look lengthy and time-consuming. We have provided possible solutions to make this easier.

## Issues to be resolved:

**1. Multiproc ingest fails for s3/s3test**

**Error:**
```python
2018-02-05 04:50:48,972 8840 agdc-ingest INFO Submitting task: (8, -21, numpy.datetime64('1990-03-02T23:11:16.000000000'))
Traceback (most recent call last):
File "/home/ubuntu/anaconda3/envs/g36/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/queues.py", line 234, in _feed
	    obj = _ForkingPickler.dumps(obj)
	  File "/home/ubuntu/anaconda3/envs/g36/lib/python3.6/multiprocessing/reduction.py", line 51, in dumps
	    cls(buf, protocol).dump(obj)
	TypeError: can't pickle _thread.RLock objects
```
**Solution:** Fixed by cloudpickling/dill `driver` in `datacube/scripts/ingest.py`
```python
def ingest_work(config, driver, source_type, output_type, tile, tile_index):
    # pylint: disable=too-many-locals
    _LOG.info('Starting task %s', tile_index)
    from cloudpickle import loads
    driver = loads(driver)
```
```python
def process_tasks(index, config, driver, source_type, output_type, tasks, queue_size, executor):
# pylint: disable=too-many-locals

def submit_task(task):
    _LOG.info('Submitting task: %s', task['tile_index'])
    from cloudpickle import dumps
    return executor.submit(ingest_work,
                           config=config,
                           driver=dumps(driver),
                           source_type=source_type,
                           output_type=output_type,
                           **task)
```
**2. `dataset_location.uri_scheme` is always set to `file` in the database regardless of index/driver used and environment set which causes errors on load.**

**Solution - Part 1:**
Set dataset_location.uri_scheme to the index specified in datacube environement.
```python
class DatasetResource(BaseDatasetResource):
    """The s3 dataset resource extends the postgres one by writing
    additional s3 information to specific tables.
    """

    def __init__(self, db, dataset_type_resource, uri_scheme='s3+block'):
        """Initialise the data resource."""
        super(DatasetResource, self).__init__(db, dataset_type_resource)
        self.uri_scheme = uri_scheme

    def add(self, dataset, sources_policy='verify', **kwargs):

        dataset.uris = ['%s:%s' % (self.uri_scheme, uri.split(':', 1)[1]) for uri in dataset.uris] \
           if dataset.uris else []
```

**4. `config.py::38` has a `[user]` section but doesn't seem to be used. It looks like `default_environment: datacube` should be used to specify the default environment but does not appear functional.**

**Workaround:** `export DATACUBE_ENVIRONMENT=s3block_env`
**Solution:** Implement `[user] default_environment=<env>`

**5. Revert `datacube/drivers/s3/datasource.py` to use `get_data_unlabeled_mp` instead of `get_data_unlabeled`**

**6. SharedArray should use `shm` instead of `file` on linux for now**

**Reason:** Defer usage of `file` for linux until further testing/benchmarks.
**Solution:** use `shm` for linux and `file` for other os including mac. 

**7. There should be a `NetcdfReaderDriver` for consistency.**

**8. `datacube/drivers/netcdf/driver.py` should have a `PROTOCOL` field for consistency.**

e.g. `file` or `s3` or `http`

**9. `datacube/drivers/s3/driver.py`**

* s3 driver protocol should be `s3+aio`. This should really be just `s3`, for discussion during the ODC workshop.
* s3 driver format should be `aio`
* s3-test driver protocol should be `file+aio`
* s3-test driver format should be `aio`
* rename where `s3block`/`s3+block` is mentioned.

**Note:** I apologise, I said I would do this one. I spent the time investigating and finding solutions to the first 2 errors above.

**10. `docs/config_samples/ingester/ls5_nbar_albers_s3test.yaml` and `docs/config_samples/ingester/ls5_pq_albers_s3test.yaml` should have `tile_size` set to `(200000, 200000)`**

If different parameters are required for tests, it's better to duplicate the file with different parameters and including them in the test directory.

`docs/config_samples` are intended for users to grab configs with known good values as a good starting point.

## Minor:
**1. Remove `datacube/drivers/s3block_index/_schema.py` as it seems unused.**

**2. `location` in ingest yaml should be moved under storage similar to bucket as it is driver dependent.**

Storage driver dependent parameters should be in its own section.

**3. `config.py::26` `default` should be uppercase `DEFAULT` to match line 30 `[DEFAULT]`. The `configparser` docs say that "By default, section names are case sensitive but keys are not"**

**4. Currently every read/write driver is loaded when the rdr_cache/writer_cache is loaded and possible heavy driver initialisation may be performed for drivers that may not even be used in the future**

There are many solutions to this:
* Upfront loading - preload drivers specific to environment on datacube initialisation
* On-demand loading - load only required driver specific to the load request. e.g. load only the `s3+aio` read driver only when a `s3+aio` load is called and not load all read drivers.
* Two stage loading:
  - one for getting the `PROTOCOL` / `FORMAT` that doesn't call the `init()` - only gets metadata
  - one for actual load that does call the `init()` and initialises the driver

**5. `core.py::562` silently disables `use_threads` if the required modules are not present. A warning should be displayed.**

This was part of the original PR but should be addressed at some point. Be nice to make it part of this merge

**6. Make environment names consistent. i.e. all with the `_env` suffix, or without it.**

e.g. `integration_tests/test_full_ingestion.py::40` and `datacube/config.py`

## Suggestions post-PR: (This would be a team ODC effort)

**1. Index + Storage (Read/Write) module and interface** To be discussed at ODC Workshop

**2. Plugin Architecture.** To be discussed at ODC Workshop

* I want my datacube to be built with plugin a, b, c, d, e.
* Datacube default plugin is a standalone plugin.
* datacube-core only defines a list of module interfaces, plugin manager + bootstrap code.
* Plugins modules live in their own repository external to datacube-core similar to Brian K's original repository structure suggestion.

**3. nD ingest + nD block reads to be revisited at a later date.**

**4. Refined driver testing**  To be discussed at ODC Workshop

* testing of drivers defined in setup.py::entry_points on basic ingestion/retrieval tasks
* file based test driver for testing all code up until storage io

**5. Proper S3 tests** To be discussed at ODC Workshop

**6. `datacube/drivers/readers.py::71, 24` and `datacube/drivers/writers.py::52, 15`. The reason for use of the `__call__` method is not clear when a normal method such as `find_driver` could have been used and may improve legibility/maintenance.**

Thank You,
Peter, Ronnie

The `cxx11` in the missing symbol name suggests maybe that you are compiling with the new C++11 ABI, but the protobuf you are using is somehow on the old ABI. One possibility is that pip is downloading and installing a wheel that was built with the old C++11 ABI. To see if this is the case, I need to see the entire install log; e.g., blow away anything pip installed previously (`pip uninstall numpy six setuptools protobuf onnx`) and then report the FULL log when you run `pip install -v onnx`.

Another possibility is that ONNX is being built with the C++11 ABI when it shouldn't.  You can disable this by passing `CFLAGS="-D_GLIBCXX_USE_CXX11_ABI=0"` as an environment variable.

Please let us know what the problem is in the end so we can help other users. Thanks.
